hub_subtitle: Business English para Missões Internacionais e Pequenos Negócios

lesson.menu_desc: Welcome a visitor from abroad and ask and answer questions with do / does and question words -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 6 -- Meeting an International Partner
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Flávia Florêncio Ferreira | Lesson 6 -- Meeting an International Partner

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a person who comes to a place to meet people or see things
a list of the topics you will discuss in a meeting
to greet someone in a friendly way when they arrive
a business that makes or sells products
a person you invite to an event or a meeting
to tell people someone's name for the first time
a land area with its own government and people
a person you know who can help you in business

paras: Next week, Flavia has an important visitor. His name is Greg, and he is a partner from a company abroad, in the United Kingdom. He works with small businesses in his country, and he wants to learn about Sebrae. Flavia is his main contact in Brazil, so she prepares an agenda for the meeting and gets ready to welcome her guest.

First, Greg sends an email with many questions. 'What does Sebrae do?' he asks. 'Where is your head office? How many consultants do you work with? Do you help companies that sell abroad?' Flavia reads every detail and writes clear answers. She wants to introduce her work with confidence, not with a translator.

To ask a question in English, we often use 'do' or 'does.' We use 'do' with I, you, we, and they: 'Do you help small businesses?' We use 'does' with he, she, and it: 'Does Greg work abroad?' For more information, we start with a question word: what, where, who, when, or how. 'Where is the meeting?' 'Who is the visitor?' On the day of the visit, Flavia welcomes Greg, introduces her team, and answers every question with a smile.
reading.source: Alumni by Better — A2 reading about welcoming an international partner

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Greg lives in Brazil and never asks questions about Flavia's work.
Flavia gets ready to welcome a partner from abroad and to ask and answer questions with do, does, and question words.
The text explains how to write a long report about the United Kingdom.

items: Greg is a partner from a company abroad, in the United Kingdom.
The text says: 'he is a partner from a company abroad, in the United Kingdom.'
Flavia needs a translator to answer Greg's questions.
The text says she wants to introduce her work 'with confidence, not with a translator.'
We use 'does' with he, she, and it.
The text says: 'We use "does" with he, she, and it.'
Greg sends an email with only one short question.
The text says Greg 'sends an email with many questions.'

gapfill.text: Remember: do = with I, you, we, they (Do you work here?). does = with he, she, it (Does she work here?). Question words open the question: what, where, who, when, how.

bank.label: Useful sentences to welcome a partner and ask and answer questions
items: Welcome to Sebrae. It is a pleasure to meet you.
Let me introduce my team and our agenda for today.
Where are you from, and how long is your visit?
Do you work with small businesses in your country?
We help companies that want to sell abroad.
Who is your main contact here in Brazil?
Please tell me one more detail about your project.

lf.title: Do, does, or a question word?

lf.text: Use do with I, you, we, they; does with he, she, it. Open with what, where, who, when, or how for more information.

items.situation: A visitor arrives at your office. Welcome them and introduce yourself.
tips: Welcome to Sebrae. I am Flavia, your contact here.
It is a pleasure to meet you. Let me introduce my team.

items.situation: Ask the partner what their company does. Use 'does.'
tips: Does your company work with small businesses?

items.situation: Ask the partner where they are from. Use a question word.
tips: Which country do you work in?

items.situation: Answer this question: 'Do you help companies that sell abroad?'
tips: Yes, we do. We help companies that want to sell abroad.
Yes, we help small businesses sell to other countries.

items: Welcome a visitor and introduce your work in three sentences: the welcome, your name and role, and the agenda for today. Use your notes.
Now ask the partner three questions with do, does, and a question word: 'Where are you from?' 'What does your company do?' 'Do you work with small businesses?'
Now welcome and ask with no notes. Sound natural, not memorized.
The partner asks: 'Do you help companies that sell abroad?' Answer: 'Yes, we do. We help small businesses sell to other countries.'

answerkey.title: Reveal the model questions and answers
list: Model welcome: 'Welcome to Sebrae, Greg. I am Flavia, your main contact here. Let me introduce my team and our agenda for today.'
do = with I, you, we, they: 'Do you work with small businesses?'
does = with he, she, it: 'Does your company sell abroad?'
question words open the question: 'Where are you from?' 'What does your company do?' 'Who is your contact in Brazil?'
answerkey.note: do for I/you/we/they, does for he/she/it, and a question word (what, where, who) for more information.

listenings.text: Hi Flavia, this is Greg from the United Kingdom. Thank you for the agenda. I am very happy to visit your office next week. I have a few questions before I arrive. What time does the meeting start? Where is your head office in Sao Paulo? Do you work with companies that sell abroad? And who is my main contact on the day of the visit? Please send me the details when you can. Thank you, and see you soon.

Good morning, everyone. This is a short reminder about our visitor next week. Greg is a partner from a company abroad, in the United Kingdom. He arrives on Tuesday, and the meeting starts at ten o'clock. Please welcome him at the front desk and help him find the room. He wants to learn what we do and how we help small businesses. Flavia is his main contact, so send all the details to her. Thank you, and have a great week.

extra_audio.text: First, Flavia reads the email from Greg, the partner from abroad. Then she prepares an agenda for the meeting. Next, she writes clear answers to all his questions. On the day of the visit, she welcomes Greg at the front desk. Finally, she introduces her team and answers every question with confidence.
